entities.name: artha
entities.cite: Even a matter inferred with great effort

entities.name: anumāna
entities.cite: inference is unreliable because of something about inference itself

entities.name: anumiti
entities.cite: a matter inferred with great effort by skilled reasoners

entities.name: satya
entities.cite: the mere carefulness of an inference is no guarantee of its truth

axioms.name: aniyata
axioms.cite: careful inferences are routinely reversed by more careful ones

axioms.name: apavada
axioms.cite: overturn the result and demonstrate the opposite

axioms.name: no_terminal_expertise
axioms.cite: there is no level of expertise at which the regress terminates

denials.name: pramana
denials.cite: the mere carefulness of an inference is no guarantee of its truth

claims.name: aniyata
claims.cite: established quite otherwise by others of still greater skill

claims.name: apavada
claims.cite: is established quite otherwise by others

rejected_readings.label: expertise_guarantees_truth
rejected_readings.rendering: Greater expertise in reasoning produces reliable, stable conclusions about matters.
rejected_readings.why: Claims that increased expertise guarantees reliability, contradicting the denial that anumana is a pramana of satya. The verse shows that greater expertise produces contrary conclusions, not convergence.

rejected_readings.label: inference_completely_invalid
rejected_readings.rendering: Because inference is unreliable and subject to reversal, it has no epistemic validity whatsoever and should be abandoned entirely.
rejected_readings.why: Over-interprets fallibilism into complete invalidity. The verse shows anumana is aniyata (undetermined by expertise), not that it is invalid or avidya. The point is regress without terminal guarantee, not total rejection of inference.

notes: The verse presents epistemic fallibilism through regress: even maximally careful inference (kusala anumatr, yatnena) can be overturned by more expert reasoners (abhiyuktatara). This denies that any level of expertise terminates the possibility of reversal. The ontology captures inference (anumana) as an epistemic power, its result (anumiti) as a cognition, and its object (artha) as semantic content to be inferred.
